card.intuition: Two ways to load related data: 'Lazy' waits until you actually need it; 'Eager' loads everything up front in a single batch.
card.analogy: Packing for a trip: Eager loading is packing every possible outfit 'just in case' before you leave. Lazy loading is and 'only' buying a jacket when you arrive and realize it's cold.
card.space_complexity: Eager uses more memory initially; Lazy uses more network round-trips
when_to_use: Eager: When you know for certain you will need the related data (avoids N+1 queries)
Lazy: When the related data is large and rarely accessed
Choosing a default policy in your ORM or GraphQL resolvers
gotchas: Lazy loading inside a loop is the direct cause of the N+1 Query Problem
Eager loading too many relationships at once can result in a 'Cartesian Product' that slows down the DB and uses massive RAM

steps.label: Eager Load
steps.annotation: Eager: load all data/modules at startup. Fast after — slow initial.

steps.label: Lazy Load
steps.annotation: Lazy: load data/module only when first needed. Faster startup.

steps.label: Trade-off
steps.annotation: Lazy introduces latency on first access. Eager wastes resources for unused items.

steps.label: Hybrid
steps.annotation: Best practice: lazy load + prefetch during idle time for likely-needed items.

exercise.prompt: Identify which loading strategy to use for these scenarios.
exercise.test_code:
assert select_loading_strategy("You need to display a list of 50 users AND their 50 profile pictures") == "Eager Loading"
assert select_loading_strategy("You load a user but only 1% of users click to see their 'Privacy Settings' table") == "Lazy Loading"
print("PASS")
